Yuna was four months old when she left Korea. She had no memories of the country where she was born. She had no memories of the language her birth parents spoke. She had no memories of the food, the customs, the sounds, the smells. All she had was a Korean name that her American parents kept, and a small photo of a woman she was told was her birth mother.

Yuna grew up in a small town in the middle of America. It was a place where almost everyone was white. Her adoptive parents were white. Her adoptive sister, also from Korea, was the only other Asian person she knew. They lived on a quiet street with white neighbors. They went to a school with white children. They went to a church with white families.

When Yuna was five years old, her kindergarten teacher did an exercise. She asked all the children to stand up if they had the same hair color as their father. Most children stood up. Then she asked them to stand if they had the same eye color as their mother. Most children stood up again. Yuna sat in her chair. She looked nothing like her adoptive parents. She had black hair and dark eyes. They had blonde hair and blue eyes. She was the only child who could not match.

The teacher kept asking questions. Yuna felt her face get hot. She felt everyone looking at her. Finally, she lied. She said her father's hair was black, just so she could stand up and go to lunch with everyone else. But she knew the truth. She was different. She did not belong.

In second grade, Yuna's school had a Heritage Day. Each child was supposed to bring something from their culture. Yuna was excited. Her adoptive mother helped her make kimchi, a traditional Korean food. They prepared it carefully. They put it in a beautiful container. Yuna could not wait to share it with her classmates.

But when she brought the kimchi to school, the other children made faces. They said it smelled bad. They said it looked gross. They said they did not want to try it. Yuna felt her excitement turn to shame. They did not like her food. So she felt they did not like her. She threw the kimchi away and never brought Korean food to school again.

As Yuna got older, she tried harder and harder to fit in. She stopped speaking the few Korean words she knew. She refused to go to Korean culture camp in the summers, even though her parents wanted her to. She spent all her time with white friends. She tried to act like them. Talk like them. Dress like them. But no matter how hard she tried, she could not change her face. She could not change her eyes. She could not change the fact that she was Korean.

When Yuna was fifteen, something happened. She was watching a Korean drama on her computer late at night. The characters were speaking Korean. Yuna could not understand a single word. But she felt something strange. A pull. A longing. She stayed awake all night, frustrated that she could not understand the language of the country where she was born.

The next morning, Yuna made a decision. She would teach herself Korean. She found a website that taught the Korean alphabet. She printed out worksheets. She practiced every day after school. Her parents were surprised but supportive. They bought her Korean language books. They found a Korean tutor online.

Learning Korean was hard. The grammar was completely different from English. The sounds were difficult to make. Yuna felt like she was making no progress. But she kept going. She watched Korean dramas. She listened to Korean music. She practiced writing Korean characters. Slowly, very slowly, she started to understand.

When Yuna was twenty-two, she decided to go to Korea. She found a program that helped Korean adoptees return to their birth country. She applied. She was accepted. She bought a plane ticket. She packed her bags. She said goodbye to her family.

The flight was long. Yuna could not sleep. She was nervous. What would Korea be like? Would she feel at home? Would people accept her? Would she finally belong somewhere?

Yuna arrived in Seoul on her birthday. She thought it was symbolic. A new beginning. A rebirth. She got off the plane and walked through the airport. For the first time in her life, she was surrounded by people who looked like her. Dark hair. Dark eyes. Korean faces everywhere.

She took a taxi to her hotel. She checked in. She went to her room. She was exhausted. She fell asleep immediately.

The next morning, Yuna woke up early. She turned on the television. Every single channel showed Korean people. Korean news anchors. Korean actors. Korean singers. Korean faces everywhere. Yuna sat on the bed and cried. She had never seen herself reflected like this. In America, she was always the only Asian face. Here, she was everywhere. She was normal. She belonged.

Yuna spent the next two weeks exploring Seoul. She walked through crowded streets. She ate Korean food at small restaurants. She visited temples and palaces. She tried to speak Korean with shopkeepers and waiters. Sometimes they understood her. Sometimes they laughed at her accent. But they were always kind.

One day, Yuna took a train to the city where she was born. It was called Daejeon. She had never been there before. She had only seen the name on her adoption papers. When she stepped off the train, she looked around. Tall buildings. Mountains in the distance. People walking everywhere.

Yuna felt a strange feeling. A sense of homecoming. But also loneliness. She had no family here. No friends. No one to welcome her back. She wanted to shout to the people walking by: "I'm back! It took me twenty-two years, but I'm back! Has anyone missed me?"

But they just kept walking. They did not know her. They did not know she had been gone.

Yuna spent two hours walking through Daejeon. She did not know where to go. She did not know what to look for. But she was grateful. Now Daejeon was not just a name on a paper. It was a real place. She had been there. She had walked its streets. She had breathed its air.

Before Yuna left Korea, she decided to climb a mountain. It was called Hallasan, and it was the tallest mountain in Korea. Yuna had never climbed a mountain before. But she felt like she needed to do it. A physical challenge to match her emotional journey.

The climb was harder than Yuna expected. The path was steep. Her legs ached. Her lungs burned. She wanted to give up. But she kept going. Step by step. Breath by breath. She thought about her journey. Learning Korean. Coming to Korea. Finding herself. Each step was like another part of her story.

After six hours, Yuna reached the top. She stood there, gasping for air, looking out at the world below. She felt the wind on her face. She saw clouds swirling around her. She had done it. She had climbed the mountain. She had come back to Korea. She had reconnected with her heritage.

When it was time to leave Korea, Yuna felt her heart break. She did not want to go. On the plane, as it lifted into the sky, she looked out the window at Seoul disappearing below. She started to cry. She cried for the twenty-two years she had spent disconnected from her heritage. She cried for the language she had lost. She cried for the culture she had rejected. She cried because she was leaving a place that finally felt like home.

But she also felt something else. Hope. She had reconnected with her roots. She had learned her language. She had visited her birth country. She had seen herself reflected everywhere. She had found a piece of herself that had been missing.

Yuna knew she would come back to Korea. She knew she would keep learning Korean. She knew she would keep exploring her heritage. Because now she understood something important. She was not just American. She was not just Korean. She was both. And that was okay. That was beautiful.

Back in America, Yuna started a group for Korean adoptees. She wanted to help others reconnect with their heritage. She wanted to share what she had learned. She wanted to create a community where people like her could feel understood.

Yuna also started speaking Korean more often. She called her Korean tutor every week. She watched Korean dramas. She cooked Korean food. She celebrated Korean holidays. She was reclaiming the spaces where her ancestors had felt safe. She was reclaiming her heritage. She was reclaiming herself.

Years later, Yuna thought about her journey. She thought about the little girl who had thrown away her kimchi. She thought about the teenager who had tried so hard to be white. She thought about the young woman who had taught herself Korean. She thought about the person who had climbed a mountain in Korea.

Yuna realized that reconnecting with her heritage had not been easy. It had been painful. It had been confusing. It had been lonely. But it had also been the most important journey of her life. Because in reconnecting with her heritage, she had reconnected with herself. And that was something no one could ever take away from her.
